request.question: Who's album which reflects on his depressive state, had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976?

title: Come to Me (France Joli song)
Love Machine (The Miracles song)
Bardeux
Lean into It
Extra Texture (Read All About It)
Jeff Dawson
Right Back Where We Started From (album)
Manu Gavassi (album)
Attitudes (band)
Welcome Back (John Sebastian album)
sentences: "Come to Me" is a #1 disco hit from 1979 performed by France Joli, who had recorded it at the age of fifteen with producer, Tony Green, who composed the song and briefly sings on it.
The track also features the famed Philadelphia session vocalists, The Sweethearts of Sigma Sound.
The song was introduced on the album "France Joli", which was released in the US on 17 April 1979 on Prelude, and rose to #26.
On the Billboard Hot 100.
"Come to Me" received a major boost on 7 July when Joli performed it as a last minute replacement for Donna Summer at a concert held on Fire Island, whose estimated audience numbered 5000.
"Come to Me" began a three-week reign atop the Billboard Hot Dance Club Play on 22 September 1979.

"Come to Me" peaked at #15 on the Hot 100 and at #36 on the R&B chart, a decent showing for a new artist, but also a symptom of the increasing disenchantment of Top 40 radio stations with disco music.
"Love Machine" is a 1975 single recorded by Motown group The Miracles, taken from their album "City of Angels".
This song was a #1 Pop smash on the "Billboard Hot 100", and the biggest-selling hit single of The Miracles' career.
This single was one of two Billboard Hot 100 Top 20 hits recorded by The Miracles with Billy Griffin as lead vocalist; the other is 1973's "Do It Baby".
Griffin had replaced Miracles founder Smokey Robinson as lead singer in 1972.
The song features a growling vocal by Miracle Bobby Rogers, with group baritone Ronnie White repeating ""Yeah Baby"" throughout the song.
Bardeux is a dance duo from the 1980s.
The group was formed in 1986 and consisted of Stacy "Acacia" Smith and Tairrie B.
The group's first single "Three Time Lover", went to #10 on Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
Tairrie B left the group to pursue a solo career and was replaced by Lisa "Jaz" Teaney.
In 1988 the group released their album "Bold as Love" and their second single "Magic Carpet Ride".
The single went to #5 on Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart and #81 on the "Billboard" Hot 100.
The group performed the song on Club MTV.
"When We Kiss", the third single from the album, would turn out to be the group's biggest hit going all the way to #36 on the Billboard Hot 100.
The fourth single released from the album, "Bleeding Heart", was a top 40 hit on the Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
A fifth and last single release from that album, "Hold Me, Hold Me" did not chart.
Lean into It is the second studio album by the American rock supergroup Mr. Big, released in 1991.

The band's breakthrough release, "Lean into It" peaked at number 15 on the "Billboard" 200 charts, while the single "To Be with You" became the band's first and only song to hit number 1 on the Billboard Hot 100.
The follow-up single, "Just Take My Heart", was another Top 40 hit, peaking at number 16 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Extra Texture (Read All About It) is the sixth studio album by English musician George Harrison, released in September 1975.
It was Harrison's final album under his contract with Apple Records and EMI, and the last studio album issued by Apple.
The release came nine months after his troubled 1974 North American tour with Ravi Shankar and the poorly received "Dark Horse" album.
The melancholic mood of the recording reflects Harrison's depressed state at the criticism generated by these projects.
Jeff Dawson is a Canadian record producer, song writer, engineer, and mixer.
Vancouver-based Jeff Dawson developed and produced the breakthrough self-titled second album for Daniel Powter.
After working on Daniel's songs and helping develop the project's sound over 3 years, Warner Bros. Chairman/CEO Tom Whalley took one listen and knew he had something extraordinary.
Daniel's single "Bad Day" was #1 for 5 weeks on Billboard Hot 100 of 2006 and has become an incredible world wide hit with almost 2.2 million downloads and nearly 3 million albums sold.
Dawson produced State of Shock's hit single "Money Honey" (certified platinum in Canada) cementing Top 10 status at multiple Canadian radio formats, including Rock, CHR, and Hot AC.

Along with becoming the #1 most played/requested song at Satellite radio selling 40,000 copies prior to its US release, surpassing platinum status for digital sales with over 51,000 sold, "Money Honey" also held down the #1 post on the Canadian all formats Radio Chart for two months, spending close to a year on the Canadian Billboard Hot 100 singles Chart.

Right Back Where We Started From is the debut album by British R&B and soul music singer Maxine Nightingale The title track was first released as a single in the autumn of 1975 and reached #8 in the UK .
In the US it reached #5 in Adult Contemporary, #9 in Dance Music/Club Play, #46 in R&B singles and #2 in the Billboard Hot 100.
It was the success of this single to which prompted her to return to London to complete a Right Back Where We Started From album.
The album was released in the US early 1976 to enthusiastic reaction, reaching #65 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Another single from the album, “Gotta Be the One”, charted at #53 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Manu Gavassi is the debut album of Brazilian singer-songwriter Manu Gavassi.
The album contains thirteen tracks, the sound is defined with strong influence on teen pop and pop rock.
The first single from the album was "Garoto Errado".
The song was released in June 11, 2011 and peaked at number 22 in Brazil's Billboard Hot 100.
The second single from the album was "Planos Impossíveis" and was released in January 25, 2011.
The song received positive reviews from critics and peaked at number 26 in Brazil's Billboard Hot 100.
The album was produced by Rick Bonadio and released through Universal Music Brazil's subsidiary, Midas Music, on October 12, 2010.
Attitudes was a rock band from Los Angeles consisting of keyboardist David Foster, guitarist Danny Kortchmar, bass guitarist Paul Stallworth and drummer Jim Keltner.
Its four members came together while working as session musicians during the recording of George Harrison's album "Extra Texture (Read All About It)".
One single by the band, "Sweet Summer Music" reached #94 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 in 1976.
James Taylor later covered their single "Honey Don't Leave L.A." and that version reached #61 on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1978.
Welcome Back is an album by American singer/songwriter John Sebastian, released in 1976 (see 1976 in music).
It reached number 79 on The Billboard 200 chart.
Two singles were released from the album with the title song "Welcome Back" reaching number one on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and Adult Contemporary charts.
It also reached number 93 on the Country Singles chart.
The second single, "Hideaway" peaked at number 95 on the Billboard Hot 100.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: Who's album which reflects on his depressive state, had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976?
hop1_docs: Klique | Klique was an American R&B trio, consisting of Howard Huntsberry, Isaac Suthers and his sister, Deborah Suthers.  They released four albums, starting with "It's Winning Time" in 1981, concluding with "Love Cycles" in 1985.  Their only song to make the US "Billboard" Hot 100 chart was a cover version of Jackie Wilson's 1960 hit, "Stop Doggin' Me Around," which reached #50 on the Hot 100 and #2 on the US "Billboard" R&B chart in 1983.  In total, they had nine songs on "Billboard"' s R&B chart.

Break It to Me Gently (Aretha Franklin song) | "Break It to Me Gently" is a song written by Carole Bayer Sager and Marvin Hamlisch which was an R&B hit for Aretha Franklin in 1977.  Released from the "Sweet Passion" album, it reached Number 1 on the Hot Soul Singles chart in June 1977.  The song's success was only faintly reflected on the "Billboard" Hot 100 with a Number 85 peak, before dropping out of the Hot 100 after two weeks.  "Break It to Me Gently" would be Franklin's final Atlantic single to appear on the Hot 100 - from which she would be absent until 1980.

Jibbs | Jovan Campbell (born November 13, 1990), better known by his stage name Jibbs, is an American rapper.  He had one top ten single on the "Billboard" Hot 100, which was his debut single "Chain Hang Low".  Jibbs' only other song that charted on the "Billboard" Hot 100 is "King Kong" (featuring Chamillionaire).  Jibbs is currently working on his next studio album, "Ten Years Later", to be released in 2017.

Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song) | "Show Me the Way" is a song written by Peter Frampton, which was originally released on his 1975 album "Frampton" and as a single, but gained its highest popularity as a song from his 1976 live album "Frampton Comes Alive! ".  The song reached number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and number 10 on the UK Singles Chart, becoming his biggest hit until "I'm in You" in 1977.

Tyga discography | The discography of Tyga, an American rapper, consists of four studio albums, two compilation albums, fourteen mixtapes, eight singles (including four as a featured artist) and forty-eight music videos.  In 2008, Tyga released his first studio album, "No Introduction", on the record label Decaydance Records.  The album featured the single "Coconut Juice", which features singer Travie McCoy; the song peaked at number 94 on the US "Billboard" Hot 100, becoming Tyga's first song to appear on the chart.  In 2010, Tyga and American singer Chris Brown released the collaborative mixtape "Fan of a Fan", which included the single "Deuces": the song peaked at number 14 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and became Tyga's first song to chart on the US Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs chart, peaking at number thirty.  Tyga also appeared on the song "Loyalty", a single by fellow rapper Birdman, and collaborated with rapper Lil Wayne on the non-album single "I'm on It", both of which failed to chart on the "Billboard" Hot 100.

Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright) | "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)" is a song by Rod Stewart, recorded at Muscle Shoals Sound Studio in Sheffield, Alabama for his 1976 album "A Night on the Town".  The song became his second US chart topper on the "Billboard" Hot 100.  spending 8 weeks at #1 from November 13, 1976 - January 1, 1977, peaked at No. 5 in the UK, No. 3 in Australia and charted well in other parts of the world as well.  It was the number 1 song on "Billboard"' s 1977 year-end chart.  It became the best-selling single of 1977 in the United States.

Lil Wayne singles discography | American rapper Lil Wayne has released 200 singles – including 44 as a lead artist – and twelve promotional singles. Lil Wayne attained his first singles chart entry in 1999 as a featured artist on Hot Boys member Juvenile's single "Back That Azz Up", which peaked at number 19 on the United States "Billboard" Hot 100 and became a top ten hit on the Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs and Hot Rap Songs charts.  Wayne later released his debut solo studio album "Tha Block Is Hot" in November 1999.  Its title track and lead single, which features B.G. and Juvenile, reached number 65 on the "Billboard" Hot 100. " Lights Out" followed in December 2000 and produced the singles "Get Off the Corner", "Everything" and "Shine". " Way of Life", the lead single from Wayne's third studio album "500 Degreez", peaked at number 71 on the Hot 100 and became a top 20 hit on the Hot Rap Songs chart.  In 2004, Wayne was featured on the single "Soldier" by American girl group Destiny's Child, which became his first top ten hit on the Hot 100 and enjoyed commercial success internationally.
hop1_titles: Break It to Me Gently (Aretha Franklin song)
Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song)
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Peter Frampton — "Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song)": "from his 1976 live album 'Frampton Comes Alive!'" ; "reached number 6 on the Billboard Hot 100" (passage [4]).
  - Rod Stewart — "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)": "recorded ... for his 1976 album 'A Night on the Town'"; "became his second US chart topper on the Billboard Hot 100" (passage [6]).
  - Other artists in passages (Aretha Franklin, Klique, Jibbs, Tyga, Lil Wayne) have Hot 100 mentions but not for 1976 (passages [1],[2],[3],[5],[7]) — none mention a depressive-state album.

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - No passage links any album to "reflects on his depressive state" — that descriptive fact is absent.
  - The question's target artist is ambiguous (could be Frampton, Rod Stewart, or another artist); passages do not disambiguate.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - A source that explicitly states which artist/album "reflects on his depressive state" (album title and citation).
  - Confirmation that the album with the depressive reflection also had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976 (if that linking fact is needed).

- retrieval_direction (prioritized):
  1. Fetch the album pages: "A Night on the Town (Rod Stewart album)" and "Frampton Comes Alive!" — check for language about depressive themes.
  2. Fetch artist biography pages: "Rod Stewart" and "Peter Frampton" (biography/album commentary sections) for mention of depression or an album reflecting depressive state.
  3. Search query: album + "depress" + artist name (e.g., "Rod Stewart album depress", "Peter Frampton album depress") or review articles: "album reflects on his depressive state [artist]".
  4. If above fail, search broader: "album reflects on his depressive state 1976 song Billboard Hot 100" to locate other artists/albums not in current passages.
response.hop2_query: 1976 album reflects on his depressive state Billboard Hot 100 song
hop2_docs: Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright) | "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)" is a song by Rod Stewart, recorded at Muscle Shoals Sound Studio in Sheffield, Alabama for his 1976 album "A Night on the Town".  The song became his second US chart topper on the "Billboard" Hot 100.  spending 8 weeks at #1 from November 13, 1976 - January 1, 1977, peaked at No. 5 in the UK, No. 3 in Australia and charted well in other parts of the world as well.  It was the number 1 song on "Billboard"' s 1977 year-end chart.  It became the best-selling single of 1977 in the United States.

Welcome Back (John Sebastian album) | Welcome Back is an album by American singer/songwriter John Sebastian, released in 1976 (see 1976 in music).  It reached number 79 on The Billboard 200 chart.  Two singles were released from the album with the title song "Welcome Back" reaching number one on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and Adult Contemporary charts.  It also reached number 93 on the Country Singles chart.  The second single, "Hideaway" peaked at number 95 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Billboard Year-End Hot 100 singles of 1976 | This is a list of "Billboard" magazine's Top Hot 100 songs of 1976.

We Can't Hide It Anymore | "We Can't Hide It Anymore" is an American pop music song written by Barry Murphy and first recorded by Larry Santos.  It appeared on Santos' 1975 album "Casablanca" and the single reached number 36 on "Billboard" magazine's "Billboard" Hot 100 chart in April 1976.  It was also recorded by Richie Havens for his 1976 album "The End of the Beginning"; and the trio Facts of Life covered the song on their 1978 album "A Matter of Fact".

You Make Me Feel Like Dancing | "You Make Me Feel Like Dancing" is a song by the British singer Leo Sayer, taken from his 1976 album "Endless Flight".  The song reached number one on the U.S. "Billboard" Hot 100 chart, making it his first number-one single in the United States, and reached number 2 on the UK Singles Chart.  "Billboard" ranked it as the No. 13 song of 1977.  Songwriters Sayer and Vini Poncia won a Grammy Award for the song in 1978 in the category Best R&B Song.

Sir Duke | "Sir Duke" is a song composed and performed by Stevie Wonder, from his 1976 album "Songs in the Key of Life".  Released as a single in 1977, the track topped the U.S. "Billboard" Hot 100 and Black Singles charts, and reached number two in the UK Singles Chart, his joint biggest hit there at the time.  "Billboard" ranked it as the No. 18 song of 1977.

Mr. Happy Go Lucky | Mr. Happy Go Lucky is the 14th album by American singer-songwriter and musician John Mellencamp.  It was released on September 10, 1996.  It was his first album released after his heart attack in 1994.  Mellencamp's music on the album is said to reflect his brush with death.  The album was recorded in Belmont, Indiana, in Mellencamp's Belmont Mall recording studio.  The first single from the album, "Key West Intermezzo (I Saw You First)," peaked at No. 14 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and is his last Top 40 single in the United States to date. " Just Another Day" was the album's second single and stalled at No. 46 on the "Billboard" Hot 100.
hop2_titles: You Make Me Feel Like Dancing
response.summary_2: Ambiguous — possibly Peter Frampton or Rod Stewart; the passages confirm each had a 1976 Hot 100 song but do not state that either album "reflects on his depressive state." Peter Frampton — Frampton Comes Alive! includes "Show Me the Way," which reached No. 6 on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976. Rod Stewart — A Night on the Town includes "Tonight's the Night," which reached No. 1 on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976.
